bibliographic_data.title: Hierarchical Integrated Spatial Process Modeling of Monotone West Antarctic Snow Density Curves
bibliographic_data.abstract: Snow density estimates below the surface, used with airplane-acquired ice-penetrating radar measurements, give a site-specific history of snow water accumulation. Because it is infeasible to drill snow cores across all of Antarctica to measure snow density and because it is critical to understand how climatic changes are affecting the world’s largest freshwater reservoir, we develop methods that enable snow density estimation with uncertainty in regions where snow cores have not been drilled.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper develops a novel class of integrated spatial process models for interpolating monotone snow density curves, which allows extending estimates of snow density beyond the depth of original cores.
core_content.key_methodology: The authors construct the space-depth process through kernel convolutions of log-Gaussian spatial processes and use Bayesian statistics to impose monotonicity constraints on the estimated snow density curves.
outcomes: Developed models for Antarctic snow density as a function of depth below the surface that allow for spatial interpolation.
Extended snow density estimates to deeper depths than were originally drilled using integrated spatial process models.
core_content.full_text_markdown: The paper addresses the challenge of estimating snow density in Antarctica, which is crucial for understanding climate changes and ice sheet dynamics. The authors develop methods for interpolating monotone snow density curves with uncertainty in regions where snow cores have not been drilled. They use a class of integrated spatial process models constructed by kernel convolutions of log-Gaussian spatial processes to achieve this goal. This approach allows extending estimates of snow density beyond the depth of original cores, providing more accurate and comprehensive data for ice sheet mass balance studies.
